SE-12-04: evaluates practices to safely and securely collect, use and store data
SE-12-07: designs, develops and implements safe and secure programming solutions

prompts.question: Identify two distinct ways in which back-end web development contributes to the operational success of a web application.
prompts.scenario: A financial technology company is developing a web-based portal for customers to view bank balances and transfer funds securely.
prompts.markingCriteria: Award 1 mark for identifying a first valid contribution of back-end development. Award 1 mark for identifying a second distinct valid contribution of back-end development.

sampleAnswers.answer: 1. Back-end development manages efficient database interactions, ensuring that dynamic data such as inventory or user profiles is stored and retrieved accurately. 2. It implements critical server-side security measures, such as authentication logic and data encryption, to protect sensitive user information from unauthorized access.

dotPoints.description: Compare Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) to SQL

prompts.question: Compare writing database queries using raw SQL with using an Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) tool.
prompts.scenario: A development team is deciding on the technical stack for a new project. The lead developer needs to explain the trade-offs between using an ORM for rapid development versus writing raw SQL for maximum performance.
markerNotes: Students should demonstrate an understanding of both concepts.
The comparison must highlight both similarities (they both interact with a database) and differences.
Key points of comparison should include developer productivity, performance/optimisation, and the level of abstraction.
Look for examples of what a developer might write in each case (e.g., an SQL string vs. an ORM method call).
commonStudentErrors: Only describing one of the two approaches.
Failing to provide clear points of comparison (e.g., only providing separate definitions).
Making absolute statements like 'ORM is always slower' without nuance.
Not understanding that an ORM generates SQL.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Clearly explains the nature of raw SQL queries (1 mark). • Clearly explains the function of an ORM as an abstraction layer (1 mark). • Provides at least one point of comparison regarding developer experience or productivity (1 mark). • Provides at least one point of comparison regarding performance or control (1 mark). • Uses appropriate terminology and structure (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Raw SQL and Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) are two different approaches for an application to interact with a relational database, each with distinct advantages and disadvantages.

Writing raw SQL involves a developer manually writing SQL statements (e.g., SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE) as strings within the application code. This approach provides maximum control and flexibility, allowing for highly optimised and complex queries that can leverage database-specific features. The main drawback is that it can be tedious, error-prone (e.g., SQL injection vulnerabilities if not handled carefully), and it tightly couples the application to a specific database schema and vendor.

An ORM, by contrast, is an abstraction layer that maps database tables to objects in an object-oriented programming language. Instead of writing SQL, a developer interacts with objects and calls methods (e.g., user.save(), products.all()), and the ORM generates the corresponding SQL automatically. The primary advantage of an ORM is a significant boost in developer productivity and a reduction in boilerplate code. It also provides a level of database independence, as the ORM can often be configured to work with different SQL databases with minimal code changes.

The key comparison points are productivity versus performance. ORMs are generally faster for development, whereas raw SQL offers better performance because queries can be hand-tuned. Another comparison is abstraction versus control. ORMs abstract away the database, making code cleaner and more object-oriented, while raw SQL gives the developer direct control over every aspect of the database interaction.
SQL is for databases. ORM is also for databases but it's different. Developers use them to get data. SQL is harder to write.

Raw SQL is when you write the actual SQL commands yourself, like SELECT * FROM users. This gives you a lot of control over the query. An ORM is a tool that lets you work with the database using programming objects instead of writing SQL. For example, you might write User.find(1) and the ORM will create the SQL for you. An ORM is often faster to develop with because you write less code, but sometimes the SQL it creates is not as efficient as a hand-written query. So, SQL can be faster to run but slower to write.

prompts.question: Assess the risk of an ORM generating inefficient SQL queries and the strategies a developer can use to mitigate this risk.
prompts.scenario: A web application is performing poorly under load. After investigation, the development team discovers that a page which should only run two database queries is running over 100. The lead developer must explain to the team what an 'N+1 query problem' is and assess the strategies they can use to fix it within their ORM.
markerNotes: A strong assessment will make a nuanced judgement about the risk.
Look for a specific, technical example of an ORM-generated inefficiency, with the 'N+1 problem' being the classic case.
The answer must discuss specific mitigation techniques; 'eager loading' is the most direct solution to the N+1 problem.
The conclusion should reflect a mature understanding of the trade-offs involved.
commonStudentErrors: Stating that ORMs are 'bad' without providing a balanced assessment.
Unable to name or describe a specific inefficiency like the N+1 problem.
Not being able to suggest a specific mitigation strategy like 'eager loading'.
Failing to conclude that the developer's understanding is key to managing the risk.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Makes a judgement on the value of the risk, identifying it as significant but manageable (1 mark). • Provides evidence, explaining how an ORM can generate inefficient queries like the 'N+1 problem' (2 marks). • Discusses mitigation strategies such as 'eager loading' (2 marks). • Draws a warranted conclusion about the need for developers to understand the ORM's behaviour (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The risk of an Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) generating inefficient SQL queries is significant and can lead to severe application performance issues if not properly managed. My assessment is that while the risk is real, it is a manageable trade-off for the productivity gains that ORMs provide.

The most common example of this risk is the 'N+1 query problem'. A developer might write a simple line of code to fetch a list of articles and their authors, for instance. A naive ORM could translate this into one query to fetch N articles, and then N subsequent queries to fetch the author for each article, resulting in N+1 total queries. For a large number of articles, this is extremely inefficient and will cripple database performance.

However, developers can mitigate this risk with several strategies. The primary strategy is to use 'eager loading'. Instead of letting the ORM 'lazily' load the related authors one by one, the developer can explicitly instruct the ORM to fetch all the required authors in a single, efficient JOIN query at the beginning. Most modern ORMs provide methods to easily specify this. Other strategies include using query analysis tools to inspect the SQL generated by the ORM during development and, where absolute performance is critical, dropping down to raw SQL for that specific query while still using the ORM for simpler operations.

In conclusion, while ORMs introduce a performance risk by abstracting away the SQL, this risk is acceptable. The onus is on the developer to not treat the ORM as a magic box, but to understand its behaviour and use available tools and techniques like eager loading to ensure the generated queries remain efficient.
An ORM can make bad SQL that is very slow. This is a risk for the program. The developer can try to fix it by writing better code or maybe using SQL instead for that part.
ORMs are slow. You should use SQL if you want it to be fast.

prompts.question: Describe the differences between using Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) and writing raw Structured Query Language (SQL) when developing a web application.
prompts.scenario: A development team is deciding how to implement the data access layer for a new e-commerce website.
prompts.markingCriteria:
3 marks: Provides a comprehensive description of the differences, clearly contrasting the object-oriented nature of ORMs with the relational table-based nature of SQL, and/or highlighting aspects such as abstraction versus control.
2 marks: Provides a general description of the differences between ORM and SQL but may lack detail on the specific mechanisms (objects vs tables).
1 mark: Identifies a basic difference or defines one technology without a clear comparison.

sampleAnswers.answer: Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) allows developers to interact with a database using the application's native programming language (e.g., Python or Java) by manipulating objects and classes. This abstracts the underlying database structure, allowing for faster development and code that is easier to read. In contrast, writing raw SQL involves constructing specific query strings to interact directly with database tables and rows. While SQL requires knowledge of the specific database syntax, it offers granular control over query performance and optimization that an ORM might obscure.

dotPoints.description: Design, develop and implement a progressive web app (PWA)

prompts.question: Justify two specific UI/UX design choices that would improve the accessibility and inclusivity of a PWA designed to display public transport schedules.
prompts.scenario: A development team is building a new PWA for their city's public transport system. The project brief states that the app must be 'accessible and inclusive'. The lead UX designer asks the team to justify specific design choices that will meet this requirement.
markerNotes: Each justification must be specific and user-centric.
Look for an understanding of the needs of different disability groups (e.g., visual, motor).
Strong answers will propose concrete, technical or design-led solutions (high-contrast mode, keyboard navigation, ARIA labels) rather than vague ideas.
commonStudentErrors: Suggesting generic good design practices without linking them to specific accessibility needs.
Failing to justify the choice from the perspective of a user with a disability.
Proposing a feature but not explaining why it improves accessibility or inclusivity.
Providing only one valid choice.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Proposes a first clear UI/UX choice related to accessibility (1 mark). • Justifies the choice by explaining how it helps a specific group of users (2 marks). • Proposes a second clear UI/UX choice related to accessibility (1 mark). • Justifies the second choice by explaining how it helps a specific group of users (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
For a public transport PWA, two justified design choices would be implementing a high-contrast colour mode and ensuring all functionality is keyboard-navigable.

First, I would justify the inclusion of a user-selectable high-contrast mode. The default design might use subtle brand colours, but these can be very difficult to read for users with low vision or colour blindness. A high-contrast mode (e.g., black background with yellow or white text) dramatically increases legibility. This choice is justified because it directly addresses the needs of a significant portion of the population, ensuring that critical information like train times and platform numbers is clearly readable for everyone, thereby improving inclusivity.

Second, I would justify the requirement that every interactive element, from searching for a station to viewing a timetable, must be fully accessible and operable using only a keyboard. This is crucial for users with motor impairments who cannot use a mouse, as well as for screen reader users who rely on keyboard navigation (e.g., using the Tab key) to move through the interface. This choice is justified because it ensures that users who cannot use traditional pointing devices are not excluded from accessing the app's core functionality, which is a fundamental requirement for public service accessibility.

One choice is to make the text bigger so people who can't see well can read it. This is a good idea for a public transport app because lots of different people use it. Another choice is to make sure you can use it with a screen reader. This is justified because blind people need to be able to use the app too.
The app should have good colours and be easy to use for everyone.

prompts.question: Explain the role of service workers in turning a web app into a Progressive Web App (PWA) and how they enable offline functionality.
prompts.scenario: A regional news website wants users to be able to read articles even when they have no internet connection (e.g., on the train). The development team is implementing service workers for the first time.
markerNotes: Must mention background script, caching strategies (Cache API), intercepting fetch events.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing service workers with Web Workers.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Describes service worker lifecycle (2 marks) • Explains caching and fetch interception for offline use (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: A service worker is a special JavaScript script that runs in the background, separate from the web page, and acts as a programmable proxy between the web app and the network. During installation, it can precache critical assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images). On subsequent visits, it intercepts network requests via the fetch event and can serve cached responses even when offline. Strategies like 'cache-first' or 'network-first with fallback to cache' allow a PWA to function without internet connectivity, dramatically improving reliability on flaky mobile networks and enabling true app-like behaviour.

prompts.question: Describe how the implementation of Service Workers and a Web App Manifest enables the news website to function as a Progressive Web App (PWA).
prompts.scenario: A developer is tasked with updating an existing news website to become a Progressive Web App (PWA) so that users can read articles while commuting through areas with poor internet connectivity.
prompts.markingCriteria:
1 mark: Identifies the purpose of either Service Workers or the Web App Manifest.
2 marks: Outlines the roles of both Service Workers and the Web App Manifest in a PWA.
3 marks: Describes in detail how Service Workers manage network requests/caching for offline access and how the Web App Manifest defines application metadata for installability.

sampleAnswers.answer: Service Workers are event-driven scripts that run in the background, acting as a proxy between the web app and the network. They enable the news site to function offline by intercepting network requests and serving cached assets (such as HTML, CSS, and article content) when connectivity is poor. The Web App Manifest is a JSON file that provides essential metadata about the app, such as its name, icons, and theme colors. This allows the browser to 'install' the website onto the user's home screen, providing a standalone, native-app-like experience without the browser UI.

prompts.scenario: A development team is updating an existing news website to allow users to read articles while offline and install the site to their mobile home screen.
prompts.markingCriteria:

1 mark: Identifies one correct technical component.

2. A Service Worker to handle offline functionality and caching.

dotPoints.description: Apply a web-based database and construct script that executes SQL

prompts.question: Describe the purpose of an SQL INNER JOIN clause. Provide a practical example of a query that uses it to combine data from a Users table and a Posts table.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer is tasked with creating a webpage that displays a list of blog posts and the name of the author for each post. The post content is in a Posts table and the author names are in a Users table. They need to construct a single SQL query to retrieve all the necessary information.
markerNotes: The description must correctly state that it returns only the matching rows from both tables.
The example query must be syntactically correct, including the SELECT, FROM, INNER JOIN, and ON clauses.
The ON condition must correctly link the primary key of one table to the foreign key of the other.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing INNER JOIN with other join types like LEFT JOIN or UNION.
Providing an incorrect or incomplete syntax for the query.
Failing to explain that the join is based on a matching value in a common column.
Using an example with tables that have no logical relationship.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Provides key characteristics of an INNER JOIN, explaining that it combines rows from two tables (1 mark). • Correctly states that the join is based on a matching value in a common column (1 mark). • Provides a syntactically correct SQL query example (1 mark). • The example correctly joins two plausible tables on their primary/foreign key relationship (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: An INNER JOIN is used in SQL to get data from two tables at once. You use it when the tables are related. For example, if you have a Users table and a Posts table, you can join them to find out who wrote which post. The query would look like: SELECT * FROM Posts JOIN Users ON Posts.AuthorID = Users.UserID;
A join puts two tables together.

prompts.question: Distinguish between a WHERE clause and a HAVING clause in an SQL query.
prompts.scenario: A data analyst is trying to write a query to find all customers who have a total order value of more than $1000. They have tried using WHERE SUM(order_value) > 1000 but it gives a syntax error. A senior analyst needs to distinguish between the WHERE and HAVING clauses to explain why their query is failing.
markerNotes: The essential point of distinction is the timing of the filter in relation to the GROUP BY operation.
A strong answer will clearly state that WHERE acts on rows and HAVING acts on groups.
Credit answers that correctly state that HAVING can filter on the results of aggregate functions, while WHERE cannot.
commonStudentErrors: Stating they do the same thing.
Failing to explain the different timing (before vs. after aggregation).
Not mentioning that HAVING is used with GROUP BY and aggregate functions.
Providing an incorrect syntax example.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Indicates the key difference: WHERE filters rows before aggregation, while HAVING filters groups after aggregation (3 marks). • Provides a simple example or notes that HAVING must be used with GROUP BY (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: The key distinction between the WHERE clause and the HAVING clause in SQL is the stage at which they filter data. The WHERE clause is used to filter individual rows from a table before any grouping or aggregation occurs. It operates on the raw data. In contrast, the HAVING clause is used to filter entire groups of rows after they have been created by a GROUP BY clause. It can only be used in conjunction with GROUP BY and it operates on the results of aggregate functions. For example, WHERE sale_amount > 100 would filter out individual sales before they are grouped, while HAVING SUM(sale_amount) > 1000 would filter out entire groups (e.g., a whole customer's sales) only if the total sum for that group meets the criteria.
WHERE is used to filter rows. HAVING is used to filter groups. You use WHERE before GROUP BY, and you use HAVING after GROUP BY. HAVING is for when you are using functions like SUM or COUNT.
They both are for filtering in SQL.

prompts.question: Identify two different types of SQL table joins.
prompts.scenario: A database administrator is explaining to a trainee that there are multiple ways to combine data from two tables, depending on whether you want to include non-matching rows.
markerNotes: The student must simply name two distinct join types. Explanations are not required.
Acceptable answers include: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN.
commonStudentErrors: Naming only one type of join.
Confusing joins with other SQL clauses like WHERE or UNION.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Correctly names one type of SQL join (1 mark). • Correctly names a second, distinct type of SQL join (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Two different types of SQL table joins are the INNER JOIN and the LEFT JOIN (or LEFT OUTER JOIN).
Inner join and outer join.
Table joins.

prompts.question: A social media platform has three tables: Users (UserID, Username), Posts (PostID, PostContent, AuthorID), and Comments (CommentID, CommentText, PostID, CommenterID). Construct an SQL script that retrieves the CommentText and the Username of the commenter for all comments on a specific post with PostID = 123.
prompts.scenario: A back-end developer needs to create an API endpoint that will display all the comments for a given blog post. To do this, they need to construct an SQL query that retrieves the comment text from one table and the commenter's username from another table.
markerNotes: The query must successfully combine data from two tables (Comments and Users).
The ON clause must correctly link the foreign key (CommenterID) to the primary key (UserID).
The WHERE clause is essential for correctly answering the prompt.
Table aliases are good practice but not essential for marks.
commonStudentErrors: Failing to join the tables, thus being unable to retrieve the Username.
Joining on the wrong columns.
Forgetting the WHERE clause to filter for the specific post.
Selecting the wrong columns in the final output.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Correctly selects the required fields (CommentText, Username) (1 mark). • Correctly uses an INNER JOIN to link the Comments and Users tables on the correct keys (1 mark). • Correctly uses a WHERE clause to filter for the specific PostID (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
sql
SELECT
 c.CommentText,
 u.Username
FROM
 Comments c
INNER JOIN
 Users u ON c.CommenterID = u.UserID
WHERE
 c.PostID = 123;

sql
SELECT CommentText, Username
FROM Comments
JOIN Users ON Comments.CommenterID = Users.UserID
WHERE PostID = 123;
SELECT * FROM Comments WHERE PostID = 123

prompts.question: Explain the purpose of database indexing and evaluate its impact on the performance of a web application that frequently searches a large Users table by email address.
prompts.scenario: A login system is becoming slow because every login triggers a full table scan on a Users table with millions of rows.
markerNotes: Must mention B-tree, reduced search time from O(n) to O(log n), trade-off with write performance.
commonStudentErrors: Thinking indexing speeds up all queries automatically.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Explains what an index is and how it works (2 marks) • Evaluates performance improvement for reads (2 marks) • Mentions trade-offs (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: A database index is a data structure (commonly a B-tree) that provides a quick lookup mechanism for specific columns, dramatically reducing the number of rows the database engine must scan. Without an index, a query like WHERE email = 'user@example.com' requires a full table scan (O(n)). With an index on the email column, lookup becomes O(log n). For a login system with millions of users, this can reduce query time from seconds to milliseconds, vastly improving perceived performance and scalability. However, indexes consume additional storage and slow down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations because the index must be maintained. In a user authentication context where reads far outnumber writes, the performance gain overwhelmingly justifies the minor write overhead.

dotPoints.description: Observe and describe the back-end process used to manage a web request

prompts.question: Describe the lifecycle of a typical web request handled by a back-end web framework, from the moment it's received by the web server to the generation of a response.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer is learning a new back-end framework like Django or Express.js. To understand how the different parts work together, a senior developer asks them to describe the sequence of events that occurs when a user clicks a link to view a product page.
markerNotes: The description must follow a logical sequence: Request -> Routing -> Controller/View Logic -> Model Interaction -> Response.
Look for key terminology: web server, web framework, routing, controller, model, database, template, response.
Higher marks for answers that clearly separate the roles of each component in the framework.
Using a specific example (e.g., a request to '/products/123') can strengthen the answer.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a jumbled or illogical sequence of events.
Failing to use correct terminology like 'routing' or 'controller'.
Confusing the role of the web server with the web framework.
Not mentioning the interaction with the database (model layer).
prompts.markingCriteria: - Describes the initial handling by the web server and passing the request to the framework (1 mark). • Explains the role of the router in matching the URL to a specific controller/function (2 marks). • Describes the controller's role in processing the request and interacting with the model/database (2 marks). • Explains the final step of rendering a template or generating a response to be sent back to the client (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The lifecycle of a web request begins when a user's browser sends an HTTP request to a specific URL. A web server (like Nginx or Apache) first receives this request. Its role is to handle the raw network connection and forward the request to the application, which is managed by a web framework.

Once the framework receives the request, the first step is routing. The framework's router inspects the URL path (e.g., '/products/123') and matches it against a predefined list of URL patterns to determine which piece of code should handle it. This code is typically a function or method within a 'controller' or 'view'.

The matched controller function is then executed. This is where the core business logic resides. The controller might parse data from the request, like the product ID '123'. It then interacts with the 'model' layer, which is an abstraction over the database. It would ask the model to fetch the details for the product with ID 123.

After the model retrieves the data from the database and returns it to the controller, the controller's final job is to prepare the response. It might pass the product data to a template engine, which generates an HTML document. Finally, the framework wraps this HTML in an HTTP response and sends it back through the web server to the user's browser.

When you request a webpage, the server sends it to the web framework. The framework's router looks at the URL and decides which code to run. That code, the controller, gets information from the database using the model. Then it puts that information into an HTML template and sends the final page back to you.
The server gets a request and sends back a webpage.

prompts.question: A user submits a 'Contact Us' form on a website. Identify TWO distinct back-end processes that the web server performs to manage this request.
prompts.scenario: A user interacts with a web form causing data to be sent to a web server.
prompts.markingCriteria: 2 marks: Identifies two correct back-end processes (e.g., server-side validation, database interaction, sending emails, generating HTTP response). 1 mark: Identifies one correct back-end process.

sampleAnswers.answer: 1. The server executes a server-side script (e.g., PHP) to validate and sanitize the input data received from the form. 2. The server connects to a database to store the message details and generates an HTML acknowledgement page to send back to the client.

dotPoints.description: Describe how collaborative work practices between front-end and back-end developers improve the development of a web solution

prompts.question: Analyse how a well-defined API contract is a crucial collaborative work practice that improves the development of a web solution by front-end and back-end teams.
prompts.scenario: A project is experiencing significant delays because the front-end team is constantly waiting for the back-end team to finish their work before they can begin. A technical lead needs to analyse how establishing a formal API contract at the start of the project could solve this problem and improve collaboration.
markerNotes: The analysis must identify the core problem: dependencies between front-end and back-end teams.
It must explain what an API contract is (a formal agreement on the structure of API requests and responses).
The key benefit to analyse is enabling parallel development.
Credit answers that mention using mock servers or tools like OpenAPI/Swagger.
commonStudentErrors: Describing front-end and back-end roles without discussing the collaborative practice.
Failing to explain what an API contract is.
Not linking the API contract to the key benefit of parallel development.
Providing a vague answer about 'good communication' without specific technical practices.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Identifies the relationship between front-end/back-end teams and their reliance on an API (1 mark). • Examines an API contract as a formal agreement that defines the API's structure and data format (2 marks). • Analyses how this contract enables parallel development by allowing the front-end to work with a mock API (2 marks). • Draws a conclusion about how this reduces integration issues and improves efficiency (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A well-defined API contract is a critical collaborative practice that dramatically improves web development by decoupling the front-end and back-end teams. The core of the analysis is how it enables parallel development. An API contract is a formal agreement, often documented using a specification like OpenAPI, that defines exactly how the API will behave: what endpoints will be available, what data formats are required for requests, and what the structure of the responses will be.

By establishing this contract upfront, the back-end team can build the API to match the specification, while the front-end team can immediately start development using a 'mock' server that returns dummy data conforming to the same contract. This eliminates the traditional waterfall-style dependency where the front-end must wait for the back-end to be complete. Both teams can work simultaneously, which significantly accelerates the overall project timeline.

Furthermore, this practice reduces integration problems. Because both teams have been building towards the same, unambiguous specification, the final integration of the front-end with the real back-end is much smoother, with fewer surprises and bugs. In conclusion, the API contract acts as the 'single source of truth' that allows two separate teams to work independently but cohesively, leading to a faster and more reliable development process.

An API contract is an agreement between the front-end and back-end teams on what the API will look like. It explains what data to send and what data you get back. This is good because it lets the front-end team start working right away with a fake API, without having to wait for the back-end team to finish. This makes development faster. It also helps because everyone agrees on how it should work, so there are fewer bugs when they put it all together.
Good collaboration means the front-end and back-end developers talk to each other so they can build the website.

prompts.question: Describe how collaborative work practices between front-end and back-end developers improve the development of a web solution.
prompts.scenario: A software team is building a complex e-commerce application. The project manager has established a workflow where front-end developers (responsible for the User Interface) and back-end developers (responsible for server logic and databases) hold daily stand-up meetings and co-design the API specifications before writing code.
prompts.markingCriteria:
3 Marks: Provides a comprehensive description of how collaborative practices lead to improvements, linking specific practices (e.g., API design, communication) to specific outcomes (e.g., data consistency, reduced errors).
2 Marks: Sketches in general terms how collaboration aids development but lacks specific examples or detailed links to improvements.
1 Mark: Identifies a benefit of collaboration or a work practice but does not describe the relationship between them.

sampleAnswers.answer: Collaborative work practices, such as agreeing on API end-points and data formats (like JSON) prior to implementation, ensure that the front-end effectively communicates with the server-side logic. By using version control systems and continuous integration tools together, front-end and back-end developers can work in parallel and detect integration errors early. This collaboration improves the solution by ensuring data consistency between the user interface and the database, reducing development time, and creating a seamless user experience where the visual design matches the functional capabilities of the system.

dotPoints.description: Develop a web application using an appropriate scripting language with shell scripts to make files and directories, and searching for text in a text file

prompts.question: Construct a shell script command to search for all occurrences of the error code '500' within a web server log file named 'server.log' and save the output to a new file named 'errors.txt'.
prompts.scenario: A system administrator needs to quickly isolate critical server errors from a massive log file to investigate a crash.
markerNotes: Command: grep
Search term: '500'
File: server.log
Redirection: > errors.txt
prompts.markingCriteria: - Correct use of 'grep' command (1 mark) • Correct syntax for search term and source file (1 mark) • Correct use of redirection operator '>' to save to file (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
`grep "500" server.log > errors.txt`

This command uses `grep` to search for the string "500" inside `server.log` and redirects the output (using `>`) to create (or overwrite) a file named `errors.txt` containing only the matching lines.

prompts.question: Explain how a shell script could be used to automate the deployment process of a web application. Include at least two specific tasks the script might perform.
prompts.scenario: A developer is tired of manually typing five different commands every time they want to update the live website. They decide to write a `deploy.sh` script.
markerNotes: Tasks: Git pull, installing dependencies, running tests, restarting server, backing up DB.
Benefit: Consistency, speed, reduced human error.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Explains the purpose of the script (grouping commands for automation) (1 mark) • Describes Task 1 (e.g. pulling code from git) (1 mark) • Describes Task 2 (e.g. restarting web server) (1 mark) • Links to benefit (efficiency/reliability) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A shell script automates the deployment by grouping a sequence of necessary terminal commands into a single executable file. This ensures the process is consistent and reduces human error. Two specific tasks might include:
1. **Updating the Codebase:** The script executes `git pull origin main` to download the latest code from the repository.
2. **Restarting Services:** The script executes `systemctl restart nginx` (or similar) to reload the web server so the changes take effect.
By running `./deploy.sh`, the developer performs complex operations reliably in seconds.

prompts.question:
A web application is designed to generate incident reports. When an administrator clicks 'Generate Report', the server must create a new directory based on the current date and find all occurrences of a specific error code within a 'daily_logs.txt' file.

Describe how the web application uses server-side scripting and shell commands to achieve this functionality.
prompts.scenario: An incident reporting feature in a web-based system management tool.
prompts.markingCriteria:
1 mark: Identifies that the server-side language (e.g., PHP, Python) must invoke system/shell commands.
1 mark: Describes the correct shell command logic for creating a directory (e.g., usage of 'mkdir').
1 mark: Describes the correct shell command logic for searching text within a file (e.g., usage of 'grep').

sampleAnswers.answer: The web application uses a server-side scripting language (such as PHP or Python) to interface with the underlying operating system. The script invokes a system execution function (such as `exec()` or `subprocess`) to run shell commands. First, it executes the `mkdir` command to create the directory using the current date. Next, it executes the `grep` command with the specific error code and filename to search 'daily_logs.txt', capturing the output for the report.

linkedOutcomes: SE-12-01: justifies methods used to plan, develop and engineer software solutions
SE-12-02: applies structural elements to develop programming code
SE-12-07: designs, develops and implements safe and secure programming solutions

dotPoints.description: Define and analyse the requirements of a problem

prompts.question: Analyse the importance of distinguishing between functional and non-functional requirements when defining a new software project. Provide an example of each for a school library management system.
prompts.scenario: A software engineer is leading the first client meeting for a new school library management system. They need to explain to the school principal how the development team will capture not just the required features, but also the performance and usability expectations for the software.
markerNotes: The analysis must explain the consequence of ignoring one type of requirement.
Examples must be clear and correctly categorised.
Look for an understanding that both are essential for project success and guide different aspects of development and testing.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing the definitions of functional and non-functional requirements.
Providing examples that are not specific to the given scenario.
Failing to analyse why the distinction is important, instead just providing definitions.
Describing a non-functional requirement as a feature (e.g., 'a login screen' instead of 'the login process must be secure').
prompts.markingCriteria: - Clearly defines functional requirements (1 mark). • Clearly defines non-functional requirements (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example of a functional requirement for the given scenario (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example of a non-functional requirement for the given scenario (1 mark). • Analyses the importance of separating them, linking it to project success, scope, and testing (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Distinguishing between functional and non-functional requirements is critically important as it provides a complete picture of the system to be built, guiding development, testing, and ensuring the final product meets user expectations. The analysis involves identifying 'what' the system must do versus 'how well' it must do it.

Functional requirements define the specific behaviours or functions of a system—the explicit actions it must perform. They are the 'what'. For a school library management system, a key functional requirement would be: 'The system shall allow a librarian to search for a book by its title, author, or ISBN.' This is a testable, specific feature.

Non-functional requirements define the qualities, constraints, and operational characteristics of a system—the 'how well'. They describe attributes like performance, security, and usability. For the same library system, a non-functional requirement would be: 'The book search function must return results within 2 seconds, even with 50 concurrent users.' This specifies a quality standard.

The importance of this analysis is that it prevents scope creep and ensures all stakeholder needs are met. Functional requirements directly inform the features developers build. Non-functional requirements dictate the system's architecture and technology choices. Without defining non-functional requirements, the team could build a system that performs all its functions correctly but is too slow, insecure, or difficult to use, resulting in project failure.
Functional is what the program does. Non-functional is other stuff. For a library, a function is to find a book. A non-function is that it has to be fast.

Functional requirements are about the features of a system, like what it must do. For a library system, a functional requirement is the ability for a student to borrow a book. Non-functional requirements are about the quality of the system. For the library system, a non-functional requirement is that the system must be easy to learn for new librarians. It's important to separate them so that developers know what features to build and also how the system should perform.

prompts.question: Describe how a data dictionary is used to define the data structures and data types for a new software system.
prompts.scenario: A new developer has joined a large, complex software project. Their team leader advises them that the first document they should study is the project's data dictionary to understand how information is structured and handled within the system.
markerNotes: A good description will identify the data dictionary as a tool for planning and documentation.
Look for examples of the types of information stored in a data dictionary beyond just the name and type (e.g., format, constraints, description).
Assess whether the student understands its role in ensuring consistency during development.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing a data dictionary with a database schema itself.
Providing an overly simplistic description that only mentions data names and types.
Failing to explain how it is used in the design process.
Not mentioning metadata.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Provides key characteristics of a data dictionary, identifying it as a central repository for metadata (1 mark). • Describes how it is used to define elements like data types, validation rules, and relationships (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: A data dictionary is a centralised repository of information about data, or 'metadata', used in a software system. Its primary use in defining data structures is to provide a detailed, unambiguous specification for every piece of data the system will handle. For each data element, the data dictionary describes key attributes. This includes its name (e.g., 'StudentID'), its data type (e.g., Integer), its size or format (e.g., 8 digits), constraints or validation rules (e.g., must be a positive number), and a description of its purpose. By documenting these details, the data dictionary ensures that all developers and stakeholders have a consistent understanding of the data, which is crucial for designing correct database schemas, data structures in the code, and user interfaces.
A data dictionary is a list of all the data. It tells you what type it is, like a number or text.

A data dictionary is a tool that describes the data in a project. It lists all the data fields and what they mean. For example, it would say that a 'FirstName' field is text and can only be 50 characters long. This helps programmers create the database.

prompts.question: Analyse the requirements for a proposed school library kiosk system, defining its boundaries, one functional requirement, one non-functional requirement, and an appropriate data structure to store book information.
prompts.scenario: A school wants to commission a student project to build a simple kiosk application for the library. Before development can begin, a detailed requirements analysis is needed to define the scope and constraints of the project.
markerNotes: Look for a clear distinction between functional (what it does) and non-functional (how it does it) requirements.
The boundaries should clearly define what is in and out of scope.
The data structure should be appropriate for the data described (e.g., a record/object/struct with typed fields).
commonStudentErrors: Confusing functional and non-functional requirements.
Defining boundaries that are too vague.
Suggesting an inappropriate data structure, like a simple list of names.
Failing to specify data types in the data structure.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Defines the system boundaries clearly (1 mark). • Provides a clear and appropriate functional requirement (1 mark). • Provides a clear and appropriate non-functional requirement (1 mark). • Designs a suitable data structure for a book (2 marks). • Specifies appropriate data types for the fields in the structure (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The requirements for the library kiosk system can be analysed as follows:
- **Boundaries:** The system will allow students to search for and view the status of books. It will not handle user authentication, book borrowing/returning, or administrative functions like adding new books. These are handled by the main librarian system, which the kiosk will read data from.
- **Functional Requirement:** The system must allow a user to search for books by title, author, or ISBN. The search results should display the book's title, author, and availability status (e.g., 'On Shelf', 'Checked Out').
- **Non-functional Requirement:** The system must return search results in under 2 seconds to ensure a responsive user experience.
- **Data Structure:** An appropriate data structure would be a 'Book' record or object with the following fields: `Title` (String), `Author` (String), `ISBN` (String, as it can contain characters like 'X'), `IsAvailable` (Boolean).

The system is for searching books. A functional requirement is that you can search by a book's title. A non-functional requirement is that it has to be easy to use. The data would be stored in a record with fields for the book's name and author.
The system needs to find books for students.

dotPoints.description: Investigate types of software implementation methods

prompts.question: Compare the parallel and phased methods of software implementation. Justify which method would be more appropriate for a large enterprise replacing its critical accounting system.
prompts.scenario: A large corporation is upgrading its 20-year-old accounting system to a new, modern platform. The Chief Financial Officer must choose an implementation strategy that minimises the risk of financial miscalculation or system downtime during the transition.
markerNotes: The comparison must highlight the key difference: running both systems vs. rolling out modules sequentially.
A strong justification will focus on risk mitigation as the key driver for choosing an implementation method for a critical system.
Look for an understanding of the resource cost (high for parallel) vs. time cost (high for phased).
commonStudentErrors: Confusing phased with pilot implementation.
Only describing the methods without comparing them.
Failing to justify the choice in the context of a critical system.
Recommending a high-risk approach like direct implementation.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Clearly describes parallel implementation (running both systems concurrently) (1 mark). • Clearly describes phased implementation (rolling out the new system in modules) (1 mark). • Provides a clear point of comparison (e.g., cost, risk, time) (1 mark). • Justifies the selection of the parallel method based on the critical nature of the accounting system (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Parallel implementation involves running both the old and new systems simultaneously for a period of time. All data is processed through both systems, and the results are compared to ensure the new system is operating correctly. Phased implementation involves rolling out the new system incrementally, one module or department at a time. The key difference is that parallel is a complete system changeover with a safety net, while phased is a gradual replacement.

For a critical accounting system, a parallel implementation is the most appropriate, despite its high cost. The justification is that it is the lowest-risk approach. By running both systems concurrently, the company can verify the new system's calculations and outputs against the trusted old system. If any discrepancies are found, the business can continue to rely on the old system while the issues are fixed, preventing any financial errors or operational disruption. The high cost of running two systems and duplicating data entry is a necessary price for ensuring the integrity of a mission-critical financial system.

Parallel means you run the old and new systems at the same time to check they match. Phased means you introduce the new system bit by bit. For an accounting system, you should use parallel. This is so you can check that the new system isn't making any mistakes with the money, because you can compare it to the old one that you know works.
You should do a phased implementation so you can do it one piece at a time.

dotPoints.description: Explore tools used to develop ideas and generate solutions

prompts.question: Compare the use of brainstorming and mind-mapping as tools for generating ideas in the initial phase of a software project.
prompts.scenario: A development team is kicking off a project to create a new mobile app for local community events. The project manager wants the team to use an effective technique to generate a wide range of feature ideas.
markerNotes: Look for the key difference: brainstorming for quantity of ideas, mind-mapping for structure and relationships.
A strong answer will describe the process of each technique.
The comparison should highlight the different outputs of each process (a list vs. a diagram).
commonStudentErrors: Using the terms interchangeably.
Only describing one of the techniques.
Failing to compare their strengths and weaknesses in a project context.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Clearly describes the process of brainstorming (generating a list of ideas without judgment) (1 mark). • Clearly describes the process of mind-mapping (creating a visual diagram of related concepts) (1 mark). • Provides a clear point of comparison based on their structure (linear list vs. radial diagram) (1 mark). • Compares their primary goals (quantity of ideas vs. exploring relationships between ideas) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Brainstorming and mind-mapping are both tools for idea generation but differ in their structure and primary goal. Brainstorming is a largely unstructured, group activity focused on quantity over quality. The goal is to generate a long list of potential ideas in a short amount of time, with all criticism suspended. The output is typically a simple list of phrases or concepts.

Mind-mapping, by contrast, is a visual and structured technique. It starts with a central concept and radiates outwards, with main ideas branching off the centre and sub-ideas branching off those. Its primary goal is to explore the relationships, hierarchy, and connections between ideas. The output is a non-linear diagram.

In comparison, brainstorming is often better for the very first step of generating a wide, diverse set of raw ideas. A mind-map could then be used as a second step to organise the best ideas from the brainstorming session and explore how different features might connect.

Brainstorming is when you just shout out ideas and write them all down on a list. Mind-mapping is when you draw a picture with a main idea in the middle and lines connecting to other ideas. The difference is that brainstorming is for getting lots of ideas, while mind-mapping is for organising them and seeing how they are related.
They are both ways to come up with ideas for a project.

dotPoints.description: Research and use the Agile software development approach

prompts.question: Explain why the Agile software development approach is often more suitable than the Waterfall model for projects where the client's requirements are not fully understood at the beginning and are likely to change.
prompts.scenario: A startup company wants to build a novel social media application. They have a core idea but are unsure of the specific features users will want. They have hired a project manager who needs to justify their choice of the Agile development methodology to the investors.
markerNotes: The explanation must contrast the rigidity of Waterfall with the flexibility of Agile.
Look for specific Agile concepts like iterations/sprints, customer collaboration, and continuous feedback.
The answer must be framed as a response to the problem of changing requirements.
Higher marks for answers that explain the consequences of using the wrong model for the scenario (e.g., project failure with Waterfall).
commonStudentErrors: Describing Agile and Waterfall without explaining why one is more suitable for the scenario.
Incorrectly defining the core principles of one or both methodologies.
Failing to mention the iterative nature of Agile or the role of customer feedback.
Using the terms 'fast' or 'slow' without explaining what makes them so (e.g., ability to adapt vs. rigid structure).
prompts.markingCriteria: - Describes the rigid, sequential nature of the Waterfall model (1 mark). • Describes the flexible, iterative nature of the Agile approach (1 mark). • Explains how Agile's iterative cycles (sprints) accommodate change (1 mark). • Explains how Agile's emphasis on customer collaboration helps refine requirements over time (1 mark). • Provides a concluding statement summarising the suitability of Agile for uncertainty (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The Agile approach is more suitable than the Waterfall model for projects with changing or unclear requirements because its fundamental structure is designed to embrace and manage change, whereas Waterfall is designed to resist it.

The Waterfall model is a linear, sequential approach where each phase (e.g., requirements, design, implementation, testing) must be fully completed before the next begins. This rigidity is its biggest weakness when requirements are uncertain. If a change is needed late in the process, it is extremely costly and difficult to go back to a previous phase, as the entire project plan is based on the initial, fixed requirements.

In contrast, the Agile approach is iterative and incremental. It breaks the project down into small, manageable cycles called 'sprints' (typically 2-4 weeks long). At the start of each sprint, the team works with the client to prioritise a small set of features from the backlog. They then design, build, and test this small increment of the software. At the end of the sprint, they deliver a working piece of the product and gather feedback from the client. This continuous feedback loop is the key reason Agile excels with uncertainty. It allows the client's understanding of their own needs to evolve, and the development team can adapt to new or changed requirements in the very next sprint. This principle of customer collaboration over contract negotiation ensures the final product is what the client actually needs, not just what was specified months or years earlier.
Agile is better because it's fast. Waterfall is old and slow. If the client changes their mind, you can fix it in Agile but you can't in Waterfall.

The Waterfall model is a very structured process where you have to finish one stage before you can start the next one, like gathering all requirements first. This is bad if the requirements change. The Agile approach is better for this because it works in short cycles called sprints. In each sprint, you can build a small part of the project and show it to the client. This lets the client give feedback and make changes along the way. Therefore, Agile is more flexible and better when you don't know everything at the start.

prompts.question: Assess the challenges a team might face when transitioning from a Waterfall to an Agile software development approach.
prompts.scenario: A large, traditional corporation has been developing software using the Waterfall method for 20 years. A new CTO is hired and mandates that all new projects will use the Agile approach. The project managers need to prepare a presentation on the potential challenges the teams will face during this transition.
markerNotes: A strong assessment will focus on the cultural and mindset changes as the primary challenges.
Look for an understanding of the shift in roles and responsibilities for both developers and stakeholders.
The answer should make a clear judgement about the difficulty and nature of the transition.
commonStudentErrors: Simply listing Agile ceremonies without explaining why they are a challenge to adopt.
Focusing on trivial issues rather than the core mindset and cultural shifts.
Not making a clear judgement on the nature of the challenges ('assess').
Failing to consider the perspective of both the development team and the client.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Makes a judgement on the significance of the challenges (e.g., 'significant and primarily cultural') (1 mark). • Provides evidence, discussing challenges related to mindset shifts (e.g., from 'big upfront design' to emergent design) (2 marks). • Provides evidence, discussing challenges related to stakeholder and client management (e.g., need for continuous engagement) (2 marks). • Draws a warranted conclusion about the nature of the transition (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The challenges a team faces when transitioning from Waterfall to Agile are significant and, in my assessment, are more cultural and organisational than purely technical. While learning new tools is straightforward, unlearning old habits and embracing a new mindset is the primary obstacle.

One major challenge is the shift from a culture of extensive upfront planning to one of iterative, emergent design. Developers and project managers accustomed to Waterfall are often uncomfortable starting a project without a complete, detailed specification. They may resist the perceived 'chaos' of Agile, where requirements are expected to evolve, and this requires a fundamental change in mindset towards embracing uncertainty and valuing adaptability over predictability.

Another significant challenge lies in stakeholder and client management. The Waterfall model often involves limited client contact after the initial requirements phase. In contrast, Agile demands continuous collaboration, with the client or product owner being an active participant in every sprint. This can be a major challenge if the client is not accustomed to this level of engagement or does not have the time to commit. The team must learn new skills in communication, negotiation, and managing stakeholder expectations on an ongoing basis.

In conclusion, the transition to Agile is a difficult process. The key challenges are not in adopting new practices like daily stand-ups, but in fostering a culture of trust, collaboration, and adaptability among the team and its stakeholders. Success depends on managing this human element of change.

It is hard to change from Waterfall to Agile. In Waterfall you plan everything first, but in Agile you don't. This can be confusing for the team. Also, the client has to be available all the time in Agile which they might not like.
Changing is hard. People don't like to change how they do their job.

prompts.question: Justify the selection of the Waterfall software development approach for a project to develop flight control software for a new commercial airliner.
prompts.scenario: A software engineering company has won a contract to develop the flight control software for a new aircraft. During a planning meeting, a new graduate suggests they use the 'modern' Agile approach. A senior engineering manager must justify the decision to use the traditional Waterfall model.
markerNotes: A strong justification must be context-specific, focusing on the safety-critical nature of the project.
Look for an understanding that Waterfall's 'weaknesses' (rigidity, extensive documentation) become strengths in this scenario.
Credit answers that effectively argue against the use of Agile in this specific context.
commonStudentErrors: Incorrectly stating that Agile is always the better methodology.
Failing to justify the choice based on the specific characteristics of a safety-critical system.
Describing Waterfall but not explaining why its features are advantageous in this context.
Not contrasting it with the unsuitability of Agile to strengthen the justification.
prompts.markingCriteria: - States a clear position that Waterfall is a justifiable choice for this context (1 mark). • Provides multiple lines of reasoning, explaining that requirements for such a system are fixed and known upfront (2 marks). • Provides further reasoning, highlighting the importance of rigorous documentation and formal verification at each stage, which are strengths of Waterfall (2 marks). • Addresses the unsuitability of Agile for a safety-critical context to strengthen the argument (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The selection of the Waterfall software development approach is highly justifiable for developing flight control software, a safety-critical system where requirements are stringent and failure is not an option. The argument for Waterfall rests on its emphasis on rigour, documentation, and formal verification.

Firstly, the requirements for flight control software are precisely defined by aerospace engineering principles and regulatory bodies; they are not expected to evolve or change during development. This aligns perfectly with Waterfall's core principle of completing a comprehensive and fixed requirements phase upfront. Secondly, Waterfall's sequential nature, where each phase must be formally reviewed and signed off before the next begins, is a major strength in this context. The design must be fully verified before coding begins, and the code must be fully tested before integration. This formal, stage-gated process is essential for demonstrating safety and compliance to regulators. The extensive documentation produced at each stage provides a critical audit trail for certification.

Furthermore, an Agile approach, which embraces changing requirements and rapid iteration, would be unsuitable and dangerous here. You cannot 'iterate' on the core logic of a flight control system or discover requirements through user feedback during a flight. The need for absolute predictability and provable correctness makes the rigid, disciplined structure of Waterfall the superior and more justifiable choice.

Waterfall is a good choice for this. The reason is that flight software is very important and you can't have any bugs. In Waterfall, you have to plan everything out perfectly at the start, which is what you need for a plane. You also have to test everything fully. Agile would be bad because you can't just change things on a plane.
Agile is always better than Waterfall so you should not use Waterfall for anything.

prompts.question: Justify which software development approach (Waterfall, Agile, or WAgile) would be most appropriate for developing a nationwide, safety-critical emergency alert system. Your justification must assess the project's characteristics against the strengths and weaknesses of each methodology.
prompts.scenario: A government agency is commissioning the development of a new national emergency alert system to be used for natural disasters and other critical incidents. The project has an absolute, non-negotiable set of requirements and must undergo rigorous safety certification. The agency must justify its choice of software development methodology to a parliamentary oversight committee.
markerNotes: A top-tier answer must make a clear recommendation and then build a strong, evidence-based justification.
The justification should be based on a clear assessment of the project's unique characteristics.
The argument should not only support the chosen methodology but also explain why the alternatives are less suitable for this specific, high-risk context.
commonStudentErrors: Incorrectly recommending Agile for a safety-critical project.
Describing the methodologies but failing to justify a choice.
Not assessing the project's specific characteristics (safety-critical, fixed requirements).
Failing to construct a sustained argument that weighs the pros and cons of each approach in context.
prompts.markingCriteria: - States a clear position, likely justifying Waterfall or a heavily Waterfall-leaning WAgile model (1 mark). • Provides multiple lines of reasoning for the chosen approach, based on the safety-critical nature of the project (3 marks). • Justifies the choice by systematically assessing the unsuitability of the other approaches (e.g., why pure Agile is too risky) (4 marks). • Builds a compelling, logical case linking project characteristics to methodological strengths (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
For a nationwide, safety-critical emergency alert system, the most appropriate and justifiable development approach is the Waterfall model. This justification is based on an assessment of the project's defining characteristics: immovable requirements, the need for exhaustive documentation, and the absolute intolerance for failure.

The core requirements for such a system are determined by government regulation, telecommunications standards, and physical realities; they are not negotiable or expected to evolve. This directly aligns with Waterfall's primary strength: a comprehensive, upfront requirements definition and design phase. The system's logic must be provably correct before a single line of code is written. The rigid, sequential nature of Waterfall, where each phase is formally verified and validated before the next begins, provides the necessary discipline and rigour for a safety-critical application.

Assessing the other methodologies shows their unsuitability. A pure Agile approach, which embraces change and emergent design, is inappropriate here. The core functionality is not open to iteration or user feedback; it must be correct and reliable from the start. The risk of introducing a critical flaw in a late-stage sprint is unacceptable when lives are at stake. A WAgile (hybrid) approach could be considered, perhaps using Waterfall for the core system and Agile for a secondary, non-critical administrative interface. However, for the core safety-critical components, the argument for the disciplined, document-heavy, and verifiable process of the pure Waterfall model is overwhelmingly strong. The 'weaknesses' of Waterfall—its rigidity and slow pace—become its greatest strengths in a context where predictability and correctness are paramount.

I would justify using the Waterfall model. An emergency alert system is safety-critical, which means it must work perfectly. Waterfall is good for this because you have to define all the requirements at the start and can't change them. This is good because the requirements for this system will be very strict and won't change. You also have to do a lot of testing in Waterfall before the system is released, which is important. Agile would be bad because it is for projects where you don't know the requirements, but for this system, you know exactly what it has to do. So Waterfall is the safest and best choice.
You should use Agile because it is the best one.

dotPoints.description: Explore social and ethical issues associated with project work, including working individually, collaboratively and responding to stakeholders

prompts.question: Discuss the ethical responsibilities of a software engineer who believes a client's requested feature, while not illegal, could be used for morally questionable purposes.
prompts.scenario: A software engineer at a social media company is asked to develop a new algorithm feature that is designed to maximise user screen time by targeting users who show signs of addictive behaviour. The engineer is concerned about the ethical implications for mental health, even though the feature is legal and would be profitable for the company.
markerNotes: A high-scoring answer must explore both sides of the dilemma in a balanced way.
Look for references to professional responsibility and codes of ethics.
The discussion should move beyond a simple 'do it' or 'don't do it' to consider professional courses of action, such as raising concerns with management.
Credit answers that discuss the concept of 'dual-use' technology.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a simplistic, one-sided answer.
Failing to recognise the conflict between contractual and ethical duties.
Not referencing the concept of a professional code of ethics.
Suggesting an unprofessional response (e.g., leaking the code).
prompts.markingCriteria: - Identifies the key issue: the conflict between professional duty to the client and a broader ethical duty to society (2 marks). • Develops points for one perspective (the duty to fulfil the client's request), referencing contractual obligations (2 marks). • Develops points for the alternative perspective (the duty to refuse or raise concerns), referencing professional codes of ethics and potential societal harm (3 marks). • Provides a balanced, nuanced treatment showing an understanding of the complexity and a proposed course of action (e.g., raising concerns, seeking guidance) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A software engineer facing a request for a feature that is legal but ethically questionable is in a complex professional dilemma. The discussion revolves around the conflict between their contractual obligation to the client and their broader ethical responsibility to the public, as outlined in professional codes of ethics (e.g., from the ACM or IEEE).

From one perspective, the engineer is employed to deliver the software the client has paid for, as long as the request is legal. An argument can be made that it is the client's responsibility, not the engineer's, to decide how the software is used. The engineer's role is to implement the functionality as specified. Adhering to this view simplifies the relationship to a purely technical, contractual one.

However, from a professional ethics perspective, the engineer's responsibility does not end with the delivery of code. Professional codes of ethics explicitly state that engineers must prioritise public good, safety, and welfare. If they reasonably foresee that a feature could be used to cause significant harm (e.g., a feature in a social media app designed to promote addictive behaviour in children, or a data collection feature with invasive privacy implications), they have an ethical duty to act. This perspective argues that an engineer is not merely a 'hired hand' but a professional with a duty of care to society.

In navigating this, the engineer should not simply refuse, but should first raise their concerns professionally with their project manager or the client, explaining the potential negative consequences. If their concerns are dismissed, they may need to consult with a professional body or, in extreme cases, refuse to work on the feature. The discussion shows that the engineer's ethical responsibilities transcend simple contractual obligations, requiring them to consider the societal impact of their work.

This is an ethical problem. The engineer is paid by the client, so they should probably do what the client asks. But if the feature is bad for people, that is also a problem. The engineer should probably talk to their boss about it. Professional engineers have a code of ethics that says they should not harm people, so they might have to say no to the client.
If it's not illegal then it's fine. The engineer should just do their job.

prompts.question: Describe two strategies a project manager can use to 'empower the client' in a software development project.
prompts.scenario: A project manager is starting a new project with a client who has had negative experiences in the past, feeling like their previous software vendor ignored their input. The project manager wants to implement specific strategies to ensure this new client feels heard and empowered.
markerNotes: The strategies described must be specific and actionable.
Look for an understanding of empowerment as giving the client a degree of control and influence over the project's direction.
Strong answers will often relate to Agile principles of customer collaboration.
commonStudentErrors: Providing vague advice like 'communicate well'.
Suggesting strategies that don't specifically 'empower' the client.
Only providing one strategy.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Describes a first valid strategy (e.g., regular demos) with some detail (1.5 marks). • Describes a second, distinct valid strategy (e.g., involving them in prioritisation) with some detail (1.5 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: One key strategy is to conduct regular and frequent demonstrations of the working software, for example at the end of every Agile sprint. This provides the client with a tangible view of progress and a concrete opportunity to provide feedback, making them feel like an active partner in the development process. A second strategy is to directly involve the client in the prioritisation of work. By giving the client the authority to decide which features from the backlog are most important to build in the next iteration, the project manager empowers them to steer the project towards their most valuable business goals, ensuring the final product truly meets their needs.
A project manager can show the client the work often so they can give feedback. They can also let the client decide which features are the most important to do next.
You should talk to the client.

prompts.question: Assess the ethical issues a software engineer must consider when a stakeholder requests a feature that could be used to secretly monitor employee activity beyond what is legally required or disclosed.
prompts.scenario: During a project meeting for a new internal communication tool, a manager asks the development team to add a 'hidden feature' that logs the sentiment of all private employee-to-employee chats, to gauge 'morale'. The team's software engineer must consider their professional and ethical responsibilities.
markerNotes: The assessment must identify specific ethical principles, such as privacy, transparency, and consent.
Look for a judgement on the engineer's responsibility to question or push back on the request.
A strong answer will reference professional codes of conduct (e.g., ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics) and the principle of public interest.
The assessment should weigh the duty to the employer against the duty to users and society.
commonStudentErrors: Giving a purely personal opinion without reference to ethical frameworks.
Failing to identify specific ethical issues like privacy.
Stating that the engineer must do what the manager says.
Not making a clear judgement on the severity of the ethical breach.
prompts.markingCriteria: - Makes a clear judgement on the ethical unacceptability of the request (1 mark). • Identifies the violation of user privacy as a key ethical issue (1 mark). • Identifies the lack of transparency and consent as another key issue (1 mark). • Assesses the engineer's professional duty to raise these concerns, potentially citing a professional code of conduct (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The stakeholder's request for a secret monitoring feature presents a significant ethical conflict for a software engineer. My assessment is that implementing such a feature would be a severe breach of professional ethics. The primary ethical issue is the profound violation of employee privacy. Private communications should be considered confidential, and secret monitoring without explicit consent erodes trust and is a misuse of technology. Secondly, the request is fundamentally deceptive, violating the principle of transparency. Users of the system would not be aware they are being monitored, which prevents them from giving informed consent.

A software engineer's primary obligation, according to professional codes of conduct like the ACM Code of Ethics, is to the public and to the well-being of users. This duty outweighs the obligation to a single stakeholder or employer. Therefore, the engineer's responsibility is to not implement the feature, to clearly articulate the ethical objections to the manager, and to escalate the issue within the company if necessary. Implementing the feature would be unethical and professionally irresponsible.

This request is unethical. The main problem is privacy, because the employees don't know they are being spied on. It's also dishonest. The software engineer should not just do it because their manager told them to. They have a responsibility to think about the users. They should explain to the manager that this is a bad idea and is not fair to the employees.
The engineer should tell the manager that spying on people is wrong.

dotPoints.description: Investigate how software engineering solutions are quality assured

prompts.question: Outline the role of a 'continual checking process' in ensuring software quality.
prompts.scenario: A software team finds that they are spending a lot of time at the end of each month fixing bugs that were introduced weeks earlier. A senior developer suggests implementing a 'continual checking process' and needs to outline its role and benefits to the team.
markerNotes: Look for the key concepts: automation, frequency (on every commit), and rapid feedback.
A strong answer will link this process to the goal of catching bugs earlier in the development cycle.
Credit answers that use the term 'Continuous Integration' or 'CI'.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing continual checking with manual end-of-project testing.
Being too vague about what is being 'checked'.
Not explaining the benefits of this approach (early defect detection, rapid feedback).
prompts.markingCriteria: - Outlines the main point that it involves frequent, automated checks (1 mark). • Outlines that it provides rapid feedback to developers (1 mark). • Outlines that its purpose is to catch defects early (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: The role of a continual checking process, often implemented via a Continuous Integration (CI) server, is to automate the quality assurance process. Its main features are that it automatically runs a suite of tests every time a developer commits new code. This provides very rapid feedback to the development team, often within minutes. The core purpose is to catch integration errors, bugs, and regressions as early as possible, preventing them from being merged into the main codebase and becoming more difficult and expensive to fix later.
A continual checking process means that every time a programmer saves their code, a server automatically runs tests on it. This is to check if the new code broke anything. It finds bugs early.
You check the code a lot.

dotPoints.description: Research and use the Agile and Waterfall software development approaches
